actions.thought: K2o is a marginal hand even on the button in a 3-handed game. While some aggressive ranges might open here, it's generally better to fold and wait for stronger cards or better spots, especially on the first hand of the tournament to establish a solid image.

I have been dealt 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Even with the button folding, playing this hand out of position against the big blind is a negative expected value situation. Folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack.

Q2o is a very weak starting hand with poor playability. In a three-handed game, while positional advantage exists on the button, this hand lacks the strength to call a raise or open for 100 chips. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with K2o after the Button folded. K2o is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position post-flop. Even in a blind vs blind situation, it is better to fold this and save the chips, as K2o is often dominated and has very low playability.
I am on the Button with a very weak hand, 8-3 offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this hand is completely unplayable. Folding is the only correct decision.

Folded to me in the small blind with a strong suited connector (5d 6d). I will raise to 300 to attack the big blind and utilize the post-flop playability of my hand if called. This is a standard blind vs. blind opening strategy.

Defending the big blind against a small blind raise with Ace-Six suited is a standard play given the pot odds and positional advantage. A6s has reasonable equity against a typical small blind opening range, and calling allows me to see the flop with position without committing too many chips early in the tournament.

I have flopped trip fives on a 5c 9c 5s board. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard to build the pot and get value from hands like pocket pairs, nines, or club draws. I will bet 300, which is half the size of the pot, to maintain a balanced range and extract value.

I have the nut flush draw on a paired board, which gives me significant equity. With 300 to call into a 900 pot, the pot odds are excellent at 4:1. Calling allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity while maintaining position against the small blind. Raising here could be risky if the opponent holds a five, so a flat call is the most balanced and +EV play.

I currently hold trips (5-5-5) with a 6 kicker. The Queen of Spades on the turn is unlikely to have improved Flashlight's range significantly unless they were floating with a hand like Q-9 or Q-J/Q-10 of clubs. Given Flashlight called a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet, their range likely includes pocket pairs, nines, or club draws. I will bet 600 (half the pot) to continue building the pot and extract value from these hands while charging the draws to see the river.

With a paired board and holding the nut flush draw, I am in a solid position to continue. The current pot is 1800, and I need to call 600, giving me roughly 4:1 pot odds. My immediate equity with the flush draw is approximately 20%, which is slightly lower than the pot odds, but the implied odds of hitting the nut flush are significant. Raising here is suboptimal as it risks inflating the pot against a potential full house, so calling is the most disciplined and +EV choice to reach the river.

I have trip fives with a 6 kicker on a board that didn't change much on the river. The 4h is a brick that shouldn't have helped Flashlight's calling range. Having led both the flop and the turn, I will continue with a value bet on the river. A bet of 1200 (half the pot) is designed to get called by hands like a 9, a Queen, or mid-pocket pairs that Flashlight might have called with on previous streets. It's unlikely Flashlight has a better five or a full house given the action, so I am betting for maximum value.

The flush draw has missed, leaving me with only Ace-high on a board where the opponent has demonstrated significant strength by betting across all three streets. With the board paired and no showdown value, my hand is extremely weak against the opponent's likely range of value hands (e.g., Queens, Fives, or Nines). There is no incentive to call here as the probability of being on a bluff is low, and the pot odds do not justify chasing with such a weak holding.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the blinds while playing in position.

I am in the small blind with Ace-three suited, which is a strong enough hand to continue against a button open. A call allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good post-flop potential and nut-flush possibilities, while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

I hold pocket threes in the big blind facing a button raise and a small blind call. I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to win 700) and massive implied odds with effective stacks over 80 big blinds. This is a highly profitable spot to set-mine. I will call to see a flop, looking to hit a set and win a big pot, while easily check-folding if I miss.

I am in the small blind on a paired board (4-4-2). I hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5 for a wheel). Given I am out of position against two opponents, checking is the standard and most disciplined play to control the pot size and evaluate how the original raiser (Flash) continues on this dry board.

On a paired board of 4d-2h-4s, my pocket 3s have marginal showdown value but are extremely vulnerable to higher pocket pairs or a player holding a 4. Since I am out of position and the small blind has already checked, checking to the pre-flop raiser (Flash) is the standard and safest play. This allows me to control the pot size and evaluate his continuation betting strategy.

The flop of 4d 2h 4s is very dry and favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor. Since both Flashlight and Pro checked to me, I will place a standard continuation bet of 1/3 the pot to take down the hand now or maintain initiative. King-high still has showdown value, but a bet allows me to represent a strong pair or an overpair while pressuring their likely missed ranges.

The board is paired (4-4-2), which significantly reduces the value of most draws and makes it a dry, tricky board. I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (the wheel). The pot is 900 and Flash bet 300, offering me 4:1 pot odds (300 to win 1200). Calling is mathematically sound here as I have sufficient equity to continue, and I want to see if the turn provides an opportunity to improve or if Flash is merely C-betting air. Raising would be ill-advised given the paired board and my out-of-position status, as it would likely only be called by better hands.

With pocket threes on a 4-2-4 board, I only have an underpair to the board's fours, making my hand extremely vulnerable. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser and a call from the small blind, my hand is very likely behind. Flashlight's call indicates they have a piece of this board or a better pocket pair. Even if both opponents are bluffing or drawing, almost any turn card will be an overcard to my threes. Folding is the safest and most profitable long-term decision.

The King of Diamonds on the turn is a significant card that likely connects with Flash's pre-flop raising range. Since I am out of position with Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw, I cannot profitably lead here. Checking allows me to control the pot size and observe Flash's intent, whether he continues his aggression or takes the free card, which is the most prudent strategy given my current equity and position.

I have turned top pair with a Queen kicker on a relatively dry, paired board. After my continuation bet on the flop was called by Flashlight, the King on the turn is an excellent card for my range. Flashlight's check on the turn indicates weakness or a trapping hand (like a 4). I will bet for value, targeting pocket pairs like 55-JJ or any drawing hands they might be holding. A bet of approximately half the pot (800) is appropriate to build the pot while keeping their range wide enough to call.

The board is paired (4-4-2-K) and my Ace-high hand has very little showdown value. My primary equity comes from a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5 for a wheel), which gives me roughly 9% equity. The pot odds offered (800 to call in a 3100 total pot) require approximately 26% equity to justify a call. Since my odds are significantly worse than the required pot odds and I am out of position against an aggressive opponent who has shown consistent strength, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.
4-9 offsuit is a weak, uncoordinated hand with poor equity against any range. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play in this position to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With A9o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 applies pressure and gives me the initiative going into the flop, taking advantage of a decent Ace heads-up.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I only need to call 200 more to see a flop, T3o is an extremely weak hand with poor playability and realization of equity, especially when playing out of position. Since it is early in the tournament and I have a healthy stack, there is no reason to defend such a marginal hand.

9h6h is a suited two-gapper, which is marginal even 3-handed from the button. While raising could steal the blinds, it plays poorly post-flop against 3-bets or calls from the blinds. Folding to preserve chips early in the tournament is the safest and most solid choice.

I am in the Small Blind with 52o, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). While I could attempt to steal the pot with a raise, 52o has almost no equity if called and I would be playing out of position post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament, there is no need to play such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button is a good position to attempt a steal, I have a healthy chip lead and don't need to take unnecessary risks with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded, making this a heads-up pot against the Big Blind. KTs is a strong hand in a heads-up scenario, and raising is the preferred way to take the initiative and control the pot. A raise to 300 chips is a standard open-raise size for this stack depth, allowing me to potentially take down the blinds or set up a strong continuation-betting opportunity on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 3c, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Small Blind, this hand is simply too weak to defend or 3-bet as a bluff. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Qd 4s is a weak hand with poor connectivity and no significant equity, especially as an opener. Given my stack size and the strategy required in a 3-handed game, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With A2o in the Small Blind after the Button folds, it's a good opportunity to attempt a steal. My Ace blocks strong Ax hands from the Big Blind, and taking the initiative with a 3x raise is a standard aggressive play in a Blind vs Blind scenario.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Since I have position on the SB for the remainder of the hand and a premium holding, I will 3-bet to 900 to take the initiative, extract value, and potentially win the pot immediately.

I raised pre-flop from the small blind with A2 offsuit as a steal attempt, but the big blind 3-bet to 900. A2o is far too weak to call a 3-bet out of position, as it plays terribly post-flop and is often dominated. A 4-bet bluff is unnecessary this early in the tournament with this specific hand. Folding is the safest and most optimal play to preserve my stack.

I have 97o on the Button. This is a very weak and uncoordinated offsuit hand. Raising as a steal could be done with a wider range, but 97o is typically near the bottom of playable hands and easily dominated. Folding is the best decision to preserve my stack for better spots.

In a blind-on-blind situation after the button folds, King-Trey offsuit is strong enough to open for a raise to steal the pot or take control of the hand. I have established an aggressive image in previous hands, and a standard 3x raise to 300 chips maintains that pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the big blind with Qs 7s against a small blind open raise from Flash. Qs 7s is a very playable hand to defend from the big blind given the pot odds and position. Flash has shown aggression in previous hands, so I should be prepared for a continuation bet, but folding or 3-betting are not the optimal lines here. Calling is the standard, balanced play that allows me to see a flop in position.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board is dry and disconnected (Tc 7h 4d). This is a good spot for a continuation bet to represent a Ten or a high pocket pair. A bet of 300 (half pot) puts pressure on Flashlight's calling range, which likely includes many hands that missed this flop like small pairs or overcards.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a relatively dry, disconnected board (Tc 7h 4d). Flash is playing aggressively, and his continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot is a standard sizing that could be made with many hands, including overcards or a ten. Folding would be too weak given my pair, and raising would likely only isolate me against hands that beat me (like a ten) or force him to fold bluffs that I currently beat. Calling allows me to control the pot size and evaluate the turn, while keeping his range wide.

The Ace of Clubs on the turn is a perfect scare card for me to continue my aggression. As the pre-flop raiser and flop bettor, my range is heavily weighted toward high cards, and the Ace fits that narrative perfectly. Flashlight's flop-calling range likely consists of mid-pairs or Tens, which will find it very difficult to continue against a second barrel on an Ace. I will bet 600 (half the pot) to maintain pressure and represent the top pair or better.

The turn card, the Ace of Clubs, is a disastrous card for my hand. My pair of sevens was a marginal holding on the flop, but now it is effectively a bluff-catcher that loses to any Ace, any Ten, or any pocket pair higher than sevens. Given that my opponent has shown consistent aggression throughout the session and continuation-bet the flop, they likely have a range that connects well with this Ace. Calling would be a speculative play with very little equity, so folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fours. This is a standard opening hand in this position to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play a pot in position. I have been maintaining an aggressive image, and a raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is consistent with my previous play.

I hold King-Queen of Clubs, which is a strong premium hand in a 3-handed game. The button (Flash) has been playing aggressively, so a 3-bet is the optimal strategy to take the initiative, potentially isolate the button, and build a pot with a high-equity hand. 3-betting to 1000 is a standard size that provides sufficient pressure while allowing me to evaluate my position post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind facing an open raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. My hand, Jd5d, is far too weak to call a 3-bet, let alone contend with two opponents showing aggression. Folding is the only sound choice.

I am facing a 3-bet from Flashlight in the small blind after my button open. While pocket fours are a decent hand to open with, they perform poorly against a 3-bet range, especially when the 3-bet size is large (1,000 chips). Flashlight's stack is approximately 6,700, making the effective stack depth around 67 big blinds. To profitably 'set-mine' (call hoping to hit three-of-a-kind), I generally look for implied odds of at least 15:1. Here, I would need to call 700 more to potentially win Flashlight's remaining 5,700, which is only a 8:1 ratio. Since Flashlight has shown a tendency to play passively in previous hands, this sudden aggression likely indicates a very strong range (TT+, AQ+). Given the lack of implied odds and the strength shown by a normally passive opponent, the most disciplined play is to fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen suited, which is a premium starting hand in a short-handed game. Opening the pot with a raise is the standard, aggressive play to build equity and take control of the hand before the flop. I will raise to 300 to effectively isolate the blinds and put pressure on my opponents.
J8o is a weak hand to play from the small blind against a button open. Folding is the standard play here to conserve chips and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Flashlight opened for 3x from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, T7o is a weak hand to defend with out of position against a 3x raise. Given that Flashlight has shown aggression in recent hands (like 3-betting me in Hand #10), I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for better spots where I have either a stronger hand or positional advantage, especially since I am currently the chip leader.

With Q7s on the button in a 3-handed game, we have a reasonable hand to open-raise and attempt to steal the blinds. The hand has some playability post-flop if called, being suited and having a high card. Opening to 3x.

I have been dealt the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em, Pocket Aces. The Button (Pro) has opened for 300. To maximize value and protect my hand, I will put in a standard 3-bet. A raise to 1000 (roughly 3.3x the open) is a strong sizing that builds the pot while out of position.

My hand, 6d 5s, is weak and unsuitable for defending against a raise and a 3-bet. Facing aggression from both the button and the small blind, continuing would be negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined play.

Q7 suited was a decent hand to attempt a steal from the button, but it does not play well against a 3-bet. Flash's 3-bet to 1000 indicates strength or a re-steal, and our hand is easily dominated by better queens or pairs. It is not worth investing 700 more chips to see a flop. Folding is the most optimal play here.

I am on the button with Queen-Four offsuit. While I have a significant chip lead and have been playing aggressively in previous hands, Q4o is a very weak hand even for a three-handed game. Both opponents have stack sizes around 80-90 big blinds, meaning I don't have enough fold equity to justify a raise with such a poor holding. Folding here is the standard play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With King-Deuce offsuit in the small blind, I am out of position against the big blind. The hand has poor playability and is a weak holding, especially given my stack depth of 81 big blinds. There is no need to play a marginal hand out of position; folding is the disciplined choice here to preserve my stack for stronger opportunities.

I am in the button position with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a weak starting hand in a three-handed game. There is no reason to commit chips with this marginal holding. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-2 offsuit, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The Button has folded, and I am facing the Big Blind. There is no reason to play this hand, so folding is the optimal and standard choice.

85 suited is a marginal hand even on the Button. Given the aggressive nature of Flash in the Small Blind, who has been raising and 3-betting frequently, attempting a steal with this hand is unnecessarily risky. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve my stack.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit in the small blind, which is a premium starting hand, especially in short-handed play. After the button (Pro) folded, I am head-to-head with the big blind (Flashlight). I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand.

Facing a raise from the small blind, my hand (Qh 7d) is sufficient to defend the big blind against a likely wide opening range. With 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), this is a standard mathematical call to see a flop.

I have Ace-King offsuit on a low, uncoordinated flop (5d-2c-6s). While I haven't hit a pair, I likely still have the best hand, and this board is unlikely to have improved Flashlight's big blind calling range significantly. I will lead with a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain aggression and attempt to take down the pot immediately or see how the opponent reacts.

The board texture of 5d-2c-6s is extremely low and likely hits the opponent's raising range better than my hand, which consists only of Queen-high. With no pair, no significant equity, and no real draw to chase, calling a continuation bet here would be a mistake. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

I am on the button with AJo, which is a very strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Raising here is the standard play to build the pot and take control of the hand. My previous play shows a consistent opening size of 300, which I will maintain here to keep my range balanced while extracting value from weaker holdings the blinds might play.

Q6 offsuit is a weak hand and playing out of position against an aggressive button open is generally unprofitable. Given that I have to call 250 to play a pot of 700, and my hand is dominated by a wide range of hands that the button could be opening with, the best strategic decision is to fold and preserve my stack.
Holding 73 offsuit in the Big Blind facing a button raise is an easy fold. The hand has virtually no playability post-flop and the pot odds do not justify calling with such a weak starting hand.

Pocket tens (Th, Tc) is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Being on the button, I have the advantage of position. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size to thin the field, take control of the hand, and build value against the blinds.

Q3 suited is a weak hand, especially when facing a raise from the Button and being out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling or 3-betting here would be a losing play over the long run, so folding is the best option.

I am holding Ace-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a 3x raise to 300. Given the 3-handed context and the favorable pot odds (needing to call 200 to compete for a total pot of 650), Ace-high is a mandatory defense against a wide button opening range. I will call to see a flop and realize my equity.

On a monotone flop of 8c-3c-7c, I hold the Nut Flush Draw with the Ace of Clubs. While I could lead out, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is standard. This allows me to see how they react. If they bet, I have a clear continue with the best possible draw and overcard equity. If they check back, I get a free card to hit my flush or an Ace.

The board is extremely wet with three clubs (8c, 3c, 7c). I hold the Ten of Clubs (Tc), which acts as a blocker to the flush, but holding only one club makes me vulnerable to any opponent holding a single club. As the aggressor pre-flop, checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity without betting into a board where I am likely behind or against a range that includes many flushes. I will take the free card and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn 6s, the board is now quite connected (8c-3c-7c-6s), and I still hold the Nut Flush Draw with the Ace of Clubs. My opponent checked back the flop, which often indicates a capped range or a hand with some showdown value that didn't want to face a check-raise on a monotone board. By leading out now for about 60% of the pot, I can apply pressure to those marginal hands (like weak pairs or even total air) and potentially take the pot down immediately. If called, I still have significant equity to hit the nut flush on the river.

The board has become very coordinated with a flush and straight possibility on the turn (8c-3c-7c-6s). Facing a 400 bet into a 1050 pot, I am getting favorable pot odds of over 3:1. With a pair of Tens and the Ten of Clubs (Tc) as a flush draw blocker, I have significant equity to continue. Folding would be too conservative given the pot odds, and raising could be reckless against a potential made hand. Calling is the most balanced approach to see the river.

On the river 8s, the board is 8c-3c-7c-6s-8s. I missed my nut flush draw (holding the Ace of Clubs). Looking back at the action, Flashlight checked back the flop and then called my turn bet of 400. This suggests they likely have a marginal pair (like 7x, 6x, or a small pocket pair) or were also on a draw. Since I hold the Ac, I block the most likely missed high-club draws. Ace-high has very little showdown value here against a range that called the turn, but betting into a board that paired the 8 (making trips possible) is risky if Flashlight has any piece of the board or an 8. I will check and hope for a check-back from a missed draw, or fold if they bet, as I have no reason to believe Ace-high is the best hand if more money goes into the pot.

The board is highly coordinated with both a flush and straight present, and the river paired the eight, making full houses possible. My pair of Tens is a marginal hand that serves as a bluff catcher. Since my opponent checked to me, checking back is the most prudent decision to reach showdown without risking more chips, as betting would likely only get called by better hands while forcing inferior hands to fold.

J6 suited is a marginal hand, even on the button. While it has some suited connectivity, it lacks high card strength and is often dominated when called or 3-bet. With plenty of chips and no need to force steals with weak holdings, folding is the standard and optimal play.

I'm in the small blind with King-Seven suited (Kc 7c). The button (Pro) folded. In a blind-vs-blind situation, K7s is a strong hand that should be played aggressively to capitalize on the position and hand strength. I'll raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind (Flashlight) and attempt to win the blinds or take control of the pot early.

Defending the big blind against an aggressive small blind raiser. With pot odds of 3:1 (calling 200 to win 600), calling with 8-9 offsuit is a reasonable defensive play given that I will have the positional advantage for the remainder of the hand.

I have top pair with King-Seven of clubs on a K-T-2 rainbow board. This is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind battle. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to get value from weaker hands like a Ten, a Jack-Ten, or straight draws like Queen-Jack, and to protect my equity.

The flop of Td-Ks-2c provides no value for my 8-high hand. With no pair, no draw, and no significant equity, calling a continuation bet from the small blind player is a losing proposition. It is better to conserve my stack and fold to wait for a more favorable spot.

Holding KQo on the button in a 3-handed game is a strong opening candidate. I have a significant chip lead and have been consistently using a 3x opening size (300 chips) throughout the tournament. This play allows me to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the blinds.

I am in the small blind with 5-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the button, calling out of position is strategically poor and risks losing more chips in a difficult post-flop scenario. Given the hand strength and position, folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack.
I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, I have no reason to get involved. This is an easy fold.

I am in the button position with 9h-7c. This is a weak, unsuited hand that does not warrant an opening raise, especially against an aggressive player in the big blind like 'Flash'. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving action.

KJo is a strong starting hand, especially blind versus blind. Raising to 3x the big blind applies pressure to the Big Blind, aiming to steal the pot right here or take control of the hand going into the flop with strong broadway cards.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, giving me 2:1 pot odds. T9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard Small Blind opening range in a 3-handed game. I have the chip lead and post-flop playability with this hand.

The flop is 5c 4c 8d, which misses my King-Jack completely and connects fairly well with the Big Blind's calling range, including small pairs, suited connectors, and various flush or straight draws. C-betting on this wet board with just king-high and no strong draws is risky and likely to get called or raised by a decent portion of Flash's range. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot, evaluate his action, and easily fold to a bet without committing more chips.

I have Ten-high on a low, somewhat connected board (5c-4c-8d). The Small Blind (Pro) raised pre-flop but has now checked the flop. While I could attempt a bluff, my hand has some backdoor straight potential and overcard value. By checking back, I see a free turn card and maintain control of the pot size with a marginal hand in position. If the turn improves my hand or if Pro checks again on a non-threatening card, I can reconsider betting.

The turn brings a 9d, adding a diamond draw to an already wet board that has several straight possibilities. My King-high with no strong draws has very little equity here. Betting as a bluff is risky since Flash's flop checking range contains plenty of draws or medium pairs that he would call with. Checking is the most prudent play, allowing me to fold easily if faced with a bet.

I have turned top pair (nines) with a ten kicker on a board that is becoming quite coordinated with straight and flush draws. The Small Blind (Pro) raised pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn, which typically signals a missed hand like AK/AQ or a marginal hand that is looking to control the pot size. I should bet here to extract value from weaker hands (like an 8 or a middle pocket pair) and to charge the various draws (clubs, diamonds, or straight draws) that might be in Pro's range. A bet of 400 into the 600 pot is a standard value size.

Flash bets 400 on the turn after we both checked the flop. The board is wet (two flush draws, straight possibilities) and I only have King-high with no draws. Calling or raising here with very little equity is a losing play. The best option is to fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket nines is a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Being on the Button, I have the advantage of acting last post-flop. I should raise to build the pot for value, protect my equity against overcards, and take the betting initiative. A standard opening raise to 3 big blinds (300) is optimal here.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. This hand is significantly below the threshold for a profitable defend, especially out of position. Given my current chip lead and the weakness of the hand, folding is the most prudent play to avoid unnecessary losses.

Holding King-Seven offsuit in the Big Blind against a button raise is a difficult spot. While the pot odds offer a decent price to call, the hand has poor equity and I will be playing out of position for the remainder of the hand. With a significant stack to protect and no strong incentive to engage in a speculative pot with such a weak holding, folding is the most disciplined and prudent strategic choice.

I am on the button with Q9 suited, which is a strong opening hand in a 3-handed game. I have the chip lead and want to apply pressure while maintaining positional advantage. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard play here to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against the blinds.

I am in the small blind with a weak offsuit hand (6-9). Calling would put me out of position against the button, who has been aggressive, and I still have to worry about the big blind. This hand does not have the strength to justify a call or a raise, so folding is the prudent play to preserve my stack.

32 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a raise from the Button, I have terrible equity and playability. The pot odds do not justify playing this hand, so folding is the optimal and standard play.

I am on the button with suited connectors, which is a playable hand in a 3-handed game. Opening from the button allows me to take control of the hand and potentially steal the blinds. Raising to 250 is a standard opening size in this spot to apply pressure to the blinds.

With A3 suited in the small blind facing a button open, 3-betting is an excellent option. It applies pressure, frequently wins the pot outright, and isolates the button while holding an Ace blocker and nut flush potential.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 5c, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened from the button and Pro 3-bet from the small blind to 900. Calling 800 more with this hand into two opponents, especially after a 3-bet, is not profitable. I have a significant stack lead and there is no reason to involve myself in a large pot out of position with a marginal holding. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I opened with T6s, which is a marginal speculative hand. After getting 3-bet by the Small Blind to 900, continuing with such a weak suited connector is negative EV. Calling would be speculative and likely lead to a difficult post-flop scenario against a presumably strong 3-betting range. Therefore, I will fold and preserve my stack.
Q5o is a very weak starting hand, even from the button. I'll fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight of hearts, a very strong hand for a blind-versus-blind battle. Since the Button folded, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot, as I have been consistently doing in previous hands.

I hold Pocket Tens (Tc, Th), a strong premium pair in this heads-up scenario against the Small Blind's raise. Given Flash's aggressive opening patterns throughout the previous hands, a 3-bet is the optimal play to take control of the pot, define their range, and capitalize on the strength of my hand while I have the advantage of position.

In a blind-versus-blind scenario, Ace-Eight suited is a very strong hand. Flashlight's 3-bet to 900 is a standard defense, but my hand has excellent equity against a wide range of hands they might be 3-betting with here. Folding would be too conservative, and 4-betting might force me into a difficult decision if they shove. Calling the 600 additional chips gives me good pot odds (2:1) and allows me to see a flop with a hand that plays well post-flop while maintaining my chip lead advantage.

In this 3-bet pot, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. The flop of 6d-3h-Td is relatively dry, although it does contain a diamond draw and a Ten. My Ace-high with a backdoor heart draw has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out into the 3-better. Checking to the aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here to see how they react and to control the size of the pot.

I have flopped top set with Tc Th on a board of 6d 3h Td. This is an extremely strong hand. Since Flash checked, I have the betting lead in a 3-bet pot. I need to bet for value to start building the pot and to charge any potential flush draws or overcards, while continuing to play aggressively against Flash's range.

In this 3-bet pot, the flop of 6d-3h-Td is relatively dry. I have Ace-high and a backdoor heart draw. Flashlight's bet of 900 into a 1800 pot (half-pot) is a standard continuation bet that could represent a wide range of hands, including overcards that I currently beat or am flipping with (like KQ, KJ, or QJ). The pot odds are 3:1, meaning I need roughly 25% equity to continue profitably. Given my stack advantage and the possibility that Flashlight is C-betting their entire 3-betting range on this texture, I will call one street to see if I improve on the turn or if the opponent slows down, allowing me to potentially take the pot or reach showdown with Ace-high.

On the turn 7s, the board texture hasn't significantly changed, though it adds some potential straight draws. I still only have Ace-high with an Eight kicker. Given that Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and followed up with a half-pot bet on the flop, they are representing a strong range that likely includes pocket pairs (JJ-AA) or a Ten. I am out of position and my hand has limited showdown value and minimal improvement potential. Checking is the most disciplined play to see how Flashlight responds and to avoid bloating a pot where I am likely behind.

I hold top set (Tens) on a board of 6d 3h Td 7s. Given that Flash checked again on the turn, I have the opportunity to extract significant value from potential draws like diamond flush draws or straight draws, as well as weaker hands like top pairs or lower sets. The pot is 3600, and a bet of 1800 represents half the pot, keeping the bet size manageable relative to my remaining stack while maximizing value before the river.

In this 3-bet pot, the board has become increasingly coordinated with the 7s on the turn. Flashlight has shown significant aggression by 3-betting pre-flop and following up with half-pot bets on both the flop and the turn. My Ace-high (Ah 8h) has very little equity against a range that likely includes pocket pairs, strong Tens, or better Aces. Given that I am out of position and the pot odds (25%) do not justify a call with only three potential outs (and even those might not be clean if the opponent holds AT+), the most disciplined and profitable play is to fold and preserve my significant chip lead.

Holding Jack-Ten offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a strong candidate for an opening raise. I have been consistently opening for 300 in late position throughout the tournament. This allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play the rest of the hand with positional advantage against the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 5c, which is a very weak starting hand. Out of position against a button raise, there is no value in playing this hand. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I have pocket Kings in the Big Blind facing a button open from Flash. This is a premium hand that I definitely want to 3-bet for value. I'll make it 1000 to build the pot, extract maximum value from Flash's opening range, and take control of the hand out of position.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit and have already put 300 into the pot. The Big Blind (Pro) has three-bet to 1000. While JTo is a decent hand for an initial raise, it does not play particularly well against a large three-bet from a player who has shown aggression (e.g., Hand #23 and Hand #12). Calling 700 more puts me in a bloated pot with a hand that is likely dominated or at best a coin flip against Pro's range. Since I have a healthy stack and am early in the tournament, I will avoid this marginal spot and fold.

My hand, 9h-5h, is weak and speculative. Given the aggression observed from my opponents, Pro and Flash, playing this hand out of position post-flop would likely be difficult and expensive. Folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with J4o, which is a very weak starting hand. Flashlight folded on the button. Facing the Big Blind, this hand has poor playability and very low equity. The standard and optimal play is to fold rather than limp or raise.
7d9d is a suited one-gapper, which is a good hand to open from the Button. Raising to 300 gives me a chance to win the blinds immediately or play a decent speculative hand in position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Pro has raised to 300 from the Button. Given that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal-at-best holding, the most prudent play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

K-3 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when out of position against a button raise. Despite the decent pot odds, the long-term expected value of calling with such a dominated hand is negative. Conserving my stack by folding is the disciplined choice.

I have been dealt Pocket Queens (Qh Qd), a premium starting hand and the third-best possible pair in Texas Hold'em. Being on the button (dealer position) gives me the advantage of acting last on all subsequent streets. My goal is to build a pot and extract value from weaker hands, or potentially win the blinds immediately. Standard opening raises in this game have consistently been 3x the big blind (300 chips). I will stick to this sizing to maintain a consistent range appearance while pushing my equity with this strong hand.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Six offsuit, which is a reasonable hand to defend against a button open given the current hand history shows Flash is aggressive with pre-flop raises. Calling is the most disciplined approach to see a flop while maintaining a manageable pot size, especially since I will be out of position post-flop.

K2s is a very marginal suited hand. Facing a Button open and a Small Blind flat call, I will be out of position against two players. Playing a weak King out of position often leads to reverse implied odds, even with the suitedness. The risk outweighs the pot odds, so folding is the disciplined choice to preserve the stack.

I flopped middle pair with an Ace kicker on a board that contains a flush draw and a Jack. Since I am out of position against an aggressive button raiser, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the pot size and evaluate my opponent's intent before committing more chips with a marginal hand.

I have an overpair (QQ) on a board of J-8-6 with two spades. This is a very favorable flop for my hand, although there are straight and flush draws present. Flashlight checked, which is expected after calling pre-flop from the small blind. I will place a value bet to get calls from weaker Jacks, middle pairs, and draws. A bet of 400 is sized appropriately to build the pot while charging draws to see the next card.

I have bottom pair on a board with flush draw potential and high cards. Given Flash's aggressive style, this is a standard continuation bet that I cannot fold yet, but raising would likely force out hands I beat while isolating myself against stronger holdings. Calling is the most balanced approach to control the pot size and see the turn.

I have a pair of sixes on a board paired with Jacks and a flush draw. With my hand strength being relatively weak and being out of position, checking is the most prudent action. This allows me to control the pot size and evaluate my opponent's aggression without overcommitting with a marginal holding.

The turn card (Jack of Spades) is quite problematic for my pocket Queens. It completes the spade flush draw and puts a pair of Jacks on the board. Since Flashlight called my pre-flop raise and my flop bet, their range includes many suited connectors (which could have hit the flush) and hands with a Jack (which now have trips). By checking back, I control the pot size and avoid a potential check-raise. If Flashlight is holding a hand like 99, TT, or an 8, they are unlikely to call another bet on this board anyway. Checking allows me to see the river for free and evaluate their action then.

I have an Ace-high flush, which is the nut flush. The board is paired with Jacks, meaning a full house (JJ, 88, 66, 55) is possible but unlikely. Given that I checked the turn and the river has completed my flush, I should definitely bet for value to maximize my winnings against a Jack or a smaller flush.

The river card (5s) is a terrible card for my pocket Queens. It puts four spades on the board, meaning any single spade in Flashlight's hand now makes a flush. Additionally, the board is paired with Jacks (Jh, Js), so any Jack in Flashlight's hand makes three-of-a-kind. Given that Flashlight called my pre-flop raise and my flop bet, their range includes many suited cards and broadway hands that could easily contain a spade or a Jack. When Flashlight leads out for 800 into the 1500 pot on this 4-flush, paired board, they are representing a very strong hand. While they could be bluffing, the probability that they have at least one spade or a Jack is too high to justify a call with only a pair of Queens and no spade in my hand. Folding is the most disciplined move here to preserve my stack.

Holding Four of Diamonds and Nine of Spades is a weak starting hand with no significant equity. In a three-handed game, playing such low-ranking, uncoordinated cards from the button is not a profitable strategy. Therefore, the optimal decision is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.
I have 74o in the small blind, which is a very weak hand. The button folded, but playing 74o out of position against the big blind is not profitable. I will fold and save my chips for better spots.
J7o is a weak offsuit hand, and being the first to act on the Button 3-handed, it's not a profitable hand to steal with. Folding is the standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 6d, a strong suited connector. After the Button folds, it is just me and the Big Blind. I will put in a standard open raise to 300 to take initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately, while remaining in a good position to play post-flop if called.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, 3d-4c. Despite the relatively cheap price to call (200 chips to win a total pot of 500), this hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Defending the Big Blind with such garbage cards is generally a losing play in the long run. Folding is the most disciplined and correct strategic choice.

I am on the button with Queen-Ten offsuit in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to play from the button to exert pressure on the blinds and utilize positional advantage post-flop. I will continue my established pattern of raising to 300 chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 3-4 suited. This is a weak hand that does not play well out of position, especially against a button open when the Big Blind has yet to act. Calling here would put me in a difficult position post-flop without the betting initiative. Folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.
Facing a button raise with K3 offsuit out of position in the Big Blind. This hand is too weak and disconnected to defend or 3-bet. Folding is the optimal play.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit on the button, which is a premium starting hand. I want to build a pot and potentially isolate one of the blinds. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate for the current blind levels and table dynamics.

Facing a raise from the Button, my hand (2c 3c) is too weak to play from the Small Blind out of position. It lacks high card value, and even if it hits a draw, playing a very small suited connector out of position is unprofitable. The standard and most sensible play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h Th, a strong suited connector. Flashlight opened from the Button to 300. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. The pot odds are favorable (200 to win 450), and 9-10 suited has excellent playability. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position against an active opponent.

On a paired board of 6c 6h 8d, I have 10h 9h which provides two overcards and some backdoor straight and flush potential. As the Big Blind checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play here. This allows me to see how the opponent reacts to the board texture before committing more chips. I will check and evaluate based on their betting size if they choose to continuation bet.

The board is paired (6c, 6h, 8d), which is relatively dry and likely missed both of us. While I am the pre-flop aggressor, Ace-high is vulnerable, and betting into a paired board might not generate much fold equity if the opponent has any piece of it. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control and realize my equity for free, avoiding bloating the pot with a weak hand while still keeping my range balanced.

On the turn Ace of Clubs, the board is 6c 6h 8d Ac. I have 10-high. The Ace is a significant card that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, although Flashlight's check on the flop suggests they might not have a strong made hand yet. However, I have no showdown value and little equity. Checking is the most prudent play to see if Flashlight will check behind again or if they will represent the Ace with a bet, in which case I will likely fold.

The turn card (Ace of Clubs) gives me top pair, which is a strong holding. Given the opponent's check on the flop and again on the turn, they likely lack a strong hand. This is an excellent opportunity to bet for value and extract chips from weaker hands like mid-pairs or draws. A bet of 400, which is roughly 60% of the 650 pot, is appropriate to build the pot.

On the Turn Ace, the board is 6c 6h 8d Ac. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, checked the flop but is now betting 400 into a 650 pot. This 'delayed C-bet' on an Ace is a common way to represent top pair. I have 10-high (9h Th) with very limited equity (only a gutshot straight draw to a 7). Given the board texture and the fact that the Ace heavily favors the button's opening range, I have no reason to continue with this hand. Folding is the most prudent way to preserve my stack.
Q9s is a solid hand to open from the Button. We have position and a hand with good playability. I'll raise to 3x to try and take down the blinds or play a pot in position.

I have been dealt pocket tens (Td Th) in the small blind. This is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. The player on the button (Pro) has raised to 300. I am in the Big Blind with 5d-2d, which is an extremely weak holding. Facing an open raise and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, calling is clearly negative expected value. There is no incentive to defend this range here, especially being out of position against an aggressive player.

Facing a large 3-bet from the Small Blind, Q9s is simply too weak to continue. I am on the button with K6o. It is a standard opening candidate in a three-handed game. Given the current stack depths and my position, a raise to 300 is a standard play to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop.

Holding 3c-4h offsuit in the small blind is a clear fold. The hand has very low equity, and playing out of position against an active button raiser like Flash is a losing strategy. It is best to preserve the stack for a better spot.
I am in the big blind facing a button open raise to 300. My hand is 84 offsuit, which is extremely weak and not playable. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

5h-Tc is a weak offsuit hand with very low playability. Even on the button, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this holding against two opponents. It is a clear fold to preserve the stack for more profitable situations.

With a suited connector (56s) in the Small Blind and the Button folded, I have a great opportunity to steal the Big Blind or play a decent speculative hand post-flop. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the BB and seizes the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 84s. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I have position post-flop and the odds to call are 2:1, 84s is a very weak hand that doesn't realize its equity well. Pro has been opening frequently, but I've had success 3-betting him with better hands or taking pots away post-flop when I have at least some connectivity. On the button in a 3-handed game, A5o is well within a standard open-raising range. Having an Ace blocks premium hands from the blinds, increasing our fold equity. Opening to our standard 3x sizing of 300 chips puts pressure on the small and big blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Five of diamonds. The Button (Pro) has raised to 3x the big blind (300). K3o is a weak hand to defend with out of position, even when getting decent odds. It has poor equity against a Button open range and will be difficult to play post-flop. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am on the button with 4s3c, which is a very weak hand. Both opponents have shown some aggression in previous hands. Flash folded on the button, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. With Ace-Six offsuit in the small blind, I have a clear equity advantage over most of the Big Blind's random range. I am in the Big Blind with 8h 3s, which is an extremely weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. Holding 9-5 offsuit is extremely weak and offers poor playability, even in a 3-handed button position. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to conserve my stack for more favorable circumstances.

With 4c Td in the Small Blind, the hand is unsuited, disconnected, and completely lacks playability or high equity. Attempting to steal the Big Blind with such a weak holding out of position is a losing play in the long run. Folding is the safest and most standard choice.

Holding 6-2 offsuit, one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em, there is no justification for entering the pot. Even though I have position as the Button, the hand's equity and playability are far too low to consider a raise or a limp. Folding is the only optimal decision.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). To maintain consistency and put pressure on the Big Blind, I will raise to 300 total. This allows me to take down the pot immediately or play a pot with the initiative if called.

I am in the big blind and facing a raise from the small blind. The pot is 400, and I need to call 200 to see the flop. My hand, 5h-Th, is a playable suited connector, and getting 2:1 pot odds makes defending the big blind a standard, positive-expectation play against a frequent raiser like Flash.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a board of A-J-T. I will bet to gain value from weaker Aces, Jacks, Tens, and draws, while also protecting my hand. A bet of 300 (half pot) is standard and keeps the pot manageable while still applying pressure.

I have middle pair (Tens) on an Ace-Jack-Ten board. While this is not the strongest holding, I have position on Flash, who has been aggressive. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 300 to win a total pot of 1200). A fold would be too weak here, as Flash could be continuation betting with air, a lower pair, or a weaker Ten. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate based on his next action.

I have top pair with an Ace, although my kicker (Trey) is weak. The Turn card (Four of Hearts) is a blank and does not change the board texture significantly. I will bet 600 (half the pot) to continue extracting value from weaker hands like Jacks or Tens and to charge the various draws for the chance to see the river. If I am raised, I will have to proceed with caution given my weak kicker.

I am holding a pair of Tens on a board of Ace-Jack-Ten-Four. This board is extremely dangerous, as it hits the calling/raising range of an aggressive player like Flash very hard (many hands containing an Ace or a Jack are in his range). My hand is currently a bluff catcher, but against a bet on the turn, I have very little equity to improve and am almost certainly losing to top pair or better. Given that the board is quite connected and I am behind a significant portion of his continuation-betting range, calling here is a negative-expectation play. It is better to preserve my stack than to chase against a strong, aggressive opponent on this texture.

I am on the button with 8d 4c, which is a very weak hand even in a three-handed game. While I have been active from the button in previous hands, this particular hand lacks any significant high card value or connectivity. Given my current chip lead and the fact that there are many hands left in the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips or enter a pot with trash. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro) after the Button (Flash) folded. Holding Ace-Nine offsuit, I have a strong hand for this situation. Raising to 300 is a standard aggressive play to seize initiative, build the pot, and capitalize on my positional advantage post-flop or potentially win the pot immediately pre-flop.

We are in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten suited and facing a steal from the Small Blind. Given our strong hand and positional advantage post-flop, a 3-bet is the optimal play to punish a potentially wide opening range from the SB, build the pot, and extract value or take down the pot immediately.

I opened the action to 300 from the Small Blind, and the Big Blind (Pro) 3-bet to 900. Holding Ace-Nine offsuit, I have sufficient equity to continue. While I am out of position, folding would be too tight given the pot odds and the strength of the hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and assess how the board texture impacts both our ranges. I will call the 600 raise to keep the pot manageable.

I am out of position against a player who 3-bet pre-flop, which indicates a strong range. The board of 2c-3c-6d is low and helps ranges that contain pairs or high cards. My hand, Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw, has very little showdown value against a continuation bet. Checking is the standard play to gauge the opponent's strength and control the pot size given I am out of position.

We 3-bet pre-flop and got called, giving us the betting lead. The flop brings us the nut flush draw along with two overcards, giving us massive equity against almost any holding. A continuation bet here serves as an excellent semi-bluff; it builds the pot for the times we hit our draw, keeps the initiative, and can fold out better ace-highs or small pairs. Sizing around 1/3 of the pot (600) is standard in a 3-bet pot to put pressure on our opponent while giving ourselves a good price.

The opponent is betting 600 into a 1800 pot, giving me pot odds of 4:1 (requiring 20% equity to call). On a board of 2c-3c-6d, my Ace-high hand has marginal showdown value, but it is too early to fold. I have the 9c, providing a backdoor flush draw, and the board is disconnected enough that the opponent could easily be continuation betting with overcards. Calling is the most profitable play to realize my equity or reassess on the turn.

I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind (Pro) on a board of 2c-3c-6d-7d. My hand (Ac-9c) has a flush draw and Ace-high. Since I am out of position and the board is disconnected but potentially hitting a 3-betting range, checking is the most strategic play to control the pot size and see if my opponent checks back or bets a size I can evaluate. A semi-bluff bet is unnecessary here as I can realize my equity for free by checking.

The 7d on the turn introduces a secondary flush draw and connects with some straight draws, but we still hold the nut club draw and overcards. Double barreling here puts immense pressure on our opponent's marginal made hands (like small-to-medium pocket pairs or weak pairs) and maximizes our fold equity. By making a half-pot bet, we dictate the pace, potentially fold out better Ace-highs or pairs, and build the pot in case we hit our flush or Ace on the river.

I am facing a bet of 1500 into a pot of 3000 with a monster draw: the nut flush draw (Ace of diamonds) and an open-ended straight draw (any 4 or 5 completes the straight). My equity against almost any made hand is extremely high, likely making me a favorite or close to it. The pot odds are 3:1, which is excellent. Given the depth of stacks and the strength of my draw, calling is the most profitable play to realize my equity and keep the pot under control, though raising all-in would also be a viable aggressive alternative to maximize fold equity.

I am in the Small Blind and first to act on the river. The board is 2c-3c-6d-7d-2d, and I hold Ad-9c, which is just Ace-high and has no showdown value. My opponent (the Big Blind) has shown significant strength by 3-betting pre-flop and firing bets on every street. Betting here as a bluff is unlikely to work against a range that has been aggressive throughout. Checking is the most logical play to either see a showdown if the opponent checks back or to fold if the opponent makes a substantial bet that I cannot call.

The river brings the 2d, completing the backdoor diamond draw and pairing the board. Since we missed our nut club flush draw and don't hold any diamond blockers, a triple-barrel bluff here is very risky. Flashlight's calling range on the turn is heavy with medium pairs, straight draws (like 45), and diamond draws, many of which either improved on this river or will hero-call a shove. Because we block the missed club draws but don't block any flushes, checking and preserving our remaining 56.5 BB stack is the most prudent decision.

Pocket fives are a strong hand in a 3-handed game, particularly on the button. Raising allows me to take the initiative and play in position against the blinds. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate to define the hand and build the pot.

T8 suited is a reasonable hand, but facing a 3x raise from the Button while in the Small Blind puts me in a tough spot. Calling leaves me out of position with a marginal hand and exposes me to a squeeze from the Big Blind. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but given the stack sizes, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position.

KTo is a solid defending hand in the Big Blind against a standard Button open. I am getting 2.25:1 on a call (200 to win 450) and have a hand that flops well enough to continue. While 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while out of position and see a flop with a hand that has good high-card value.
